coredata.dc:title: A strategy to synthesize highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposite based on hydrothermal deposition method and its application in anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Zhan
Li, MengQi
Gan, Lu
Wan, Lijia
Chen, Xiaoling
Zhang, Xiaohang
Gui, Weijun
coredata.dc:description: Highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal deposition method for the first time. With the use of dialyzed CDs solution as precursor, the synthetized composite shows negligible emission intensity of CDs. On replacing the dialyzed CDs solution with the crude one, highly luminescent composite can be obtained successfully. Based on the analysis of XPS and FT-IR spectra, one can deduce that the existence of a large amount of citrate anions, ethylenediamine cations and polymer-like CDs in the crude CDs solution make it as nutrient solution of CDs, which can not only facilitate growth of CDs on the surface of GVE, but also greatly prevent nitrogen atoms from falling off the particles during the hydrothermal deposition process. For the dialyzed CDs solution, due to the ions and polymer-like CDs being thoroughly dialyzed, the coated CDs are fragile during hydrothermal deposition process. Besides, the effects of different dosages of ammonia on emission intensities of the two composites are studied. Based on the designable patterns for security application, one can think that the highly luminescent composite is one of the most promising candidates in the anti-counterfeiting field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide ions
Carbon dots
High luminescence
Hydrothermal deposition method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61961027 ) and the College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Jiangxi Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the proposed preparation and anti-counterfeiting application.

TEM (a) and High-resolution TEM (b) images of the annealed GVE nanocrystals; TEM (c, d), high-resolution TEM (e) and EDX (f) images of the GVE/cCDs composite material; XRD patterns (g) of the products aCDs, GVE and GVE/cCDs.
UV–visible absorption spectra of dCDs, GVE and GVE/cCDs (a); XPS survey spectra of GVE and GVE/cCDs (b); High-resolution XPS C1s spectra of GVE (c) and GVE/cCDs (d).

FT-IR spectra of CA, c(d)CDs, GVE and its composites (a); photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of GVE and its composites with λex = 254 nm (inset: relative total intensities of the six samples) (b) and λex = 365 nm (c), respectively; photographs of GVE/cCDs solution under excitation of 254, 365 and 254 and 365 nm UV light, respectively (d).
CIE chromaticity diagram of GVE and its composites.

The logo of “Nanchang Institute of Technology” loaded on a fluorescer-free paper using GVE/cCDs (a), GVE/cCDs-m (b) or GVE/CDs-h (c) as ink under daylight (1), 254 (2), 365 (3) and 254 and 365 (4) nm UV light, respectively.
Atomic ratios of Gd, C, N, V, O, Eu in XPS results.
CIE chromaticity coordinates (x, y) for GVE and its composites (λex = 365 nm).

head.ce:title: A strategy to synthesize highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposite based on hydrothermal deposition method and its application in anti-counterfeiting

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanchang Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Conversion and Energy Storage Materials, Nanchang 330099, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanchang Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Conversion and Energy Storage Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanchang Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Conversion and Energy Storage Materials, Nanchang, 330099, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials and New Energy Technology, Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang 330099, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang 330099, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, Nanchang Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang, 330099, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, Nanchang Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal deposition method for the first time. With the use of dialyzed CDs solution as precursor, the synthetized composite shows negligible emission intensity of CDs. On replacing the dialyzed CDs solution with the crude one, highly luminescent composite can be obtained successfully. Based on the analysis of XPS and FT-IR spectra, one can deduce that the existence of a large amount of citrate anions, ethylenediamine cations and polymer-like CDs in the crude CDs solution make it as nutrient solution of CDs, which can not only facilitate growth of CDs on the surface of GVE, but also greatly prevent nitrogen atoms from falling off the particles during the hydrothermal deposition process. For the dialyzed CDs solution, due to the ions and polymer-like CDs being thoroughly dialyzed, the coated CDs are fragile during hydrothermal deposition process. Besides, the effects of different dosages of ammonia on emission intensities of the two composites are studied. Based on the designable patterns for security application, one can think that the highly luminescent composite is one of the most promising candidates in the anti-counterfeiting field.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new member of the carbon materials family, are conventionally defined as carbon nanoparticles with size below 10 nm. Recently, fluorescent CDs have attracted much attention due to their superior properties, such as easy preparation and purification, high photostability, adjustable optical and surface properties, low toxicity, environmental friendliness and superior biocompatibility [1,2]. These exceptional performances make them useful for biosensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, light-emitting devices, photocatalysis, fluorescence probe, anti-counterfeiting and so on [3–6]. To facilitate feasibility of applications of CDs in industrial fields and daily lives, it is expected to synthesize highly luminescent CDs with various synthetic methods, including acidic oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, laser ablation, arc discharge, and microwave-assisted synthesis [7,8]. Among them, hydrothermal method has aroused great interest due to its facile and high-output strategy, which is suitable for industrial-scale production [9]. In 2013, Zhu et al. employed hydrothermal method to prepare high fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), quantum yields (QY) of which is as high as 80%, and the resultant CDs are also suitable for industrial-scale production (yield is ca. 58%) [10]. The pioneer work is expected to effectively improve practicability of CDs in terms of industrial fields and daily lives.

Nowadays, material versatility is strongly desired in an extensive variety of applications. To achieve this purpose, one feasible way is to integrate different nanostructures into a composite or hybrid material, which can effectively remedy the shortcomings of individual materials [11]. As for CDs, their composites or hybrid materials have been developed by combing CDs with different functional materials, such as luminescent materials, biomaterials, etc. [12,13]. As far as luminescent materials are concerned, the common synthetic routes to composites related to CDs are hydrothermal/solvothermal method and physical mixing process. For instance, Li et al. recently fabricated the dual-mode luminescent NaYF4: Yb, Er (Tm)/CDs composite material via a straightforward solvothermal method [14]. However, Xu et al. stated that the CDs only attached on the surface of UC materials, and could be fallen off easily [15].

To find a more suitable synthetic scheme for the composites related to CDs, we seek for the answer in the realm of photocatalysis. To markedly improve photocatalytic H2 production activity, many efforts have been focused on incorporating CDs into matrix materials with hydrothermal treatment. For example, Zhang synthesized the CDs modified P25 TiO2 composites (CDs/P25) via a facile one-step hydrothermal reaction. For the composites, CDs act as an electron reservoir to improve the efficient separation of the photoinduced electron–hole pairs of P25 [16]. The notably improved photocatalytic activity signifies efficient energy transfer (ET) between CDs and P25. In order to distinguish above scheme from the straightforward hydrothermal/solvothermal method, we call it hydrothermal deposition method as that reported in Ref. [17].

The novel synthetic scheme paves a new way to preparing highly luminescent composites related to CDs. Inspired by this scheme, we took GdVO4: 3% Eu3+ (annealed at 600 °C for 5 h) and N-CDs (dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h) as precursors, and prepared corresponding composite material. Regrettably, the synthetized nanocomposite shows negligible emission intensity of CDs. Interestingly, on replacing the dialyzed CDs solution with their crude one, highly luminescent nanocomposite can be obtained successfully via the scheme mentioned above. Obviously, this scheme can be used to synthesize other composites with similar structure, hence our results provide a general approach to preparing highly luminescent composites. To the best of our knowledge, no such report was described in any literature. The obtained highly luminescent nanocomposite was further used for security application. Based on the designable patterns, one can see that the composite is one of the most promising candidates in the anti-counterfeiting field.

Gadolinium nitrate (Gd(NO3)3.6 H2O, 99.99%), europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3.6 H2O, 99.99%), sodium orthovanadate dodecahydrate (Na3VO4.12 H2O, 99%), sodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7.2 H2O, 99%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) monohydrate (99.8%) and ethylenediamine (EDA) monohydrate (98%), PAA ((C3H4O2)n, 50% solution, MW ~ 3000) were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Besides, concentrated ammonia aqueous solution (28 wt%) and ethyl alcohol (AR) were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

The GdVO4: 3% Eu3+ nanocrystals were synthesized via hydrothermal method [18]. In a typical experiment, 2.0 mmol RE(NO3)3 (1.94 mmol Gd(NO3)3 and 0.06 mmol Eu(NO3)3), 1.5 mmol Na3VO4.12 H2O and 1.5 mmol Na3C6H5O7.2 H2O were firstly dissolved in 18 ml deionized (DI) water, respectively. Then, sodium citrate was added in the nitrate aqueous solution with continuous stirring for 1.5 h. Subsequently, the vanadate solution was introduced into the mixture. After further stirring for 1.5 h, pH of above mixture was adjusted to about 9 with 6 ml ammonium hydroxide (28%), the well-stirred suspension was then transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Once the autoclave naturally cooled down to room temperature, the as-prepared phosphor was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rmp for 5 min and washed with DI water and ethyl alcohol in turn several times before being dried at 80 °C for 8 h. The obtained powder was finally annealed at 600 °C for 5 h in muffle furnace.
In order to ensure the comparability of spectral test results, 0.12 g of annealed GdVO4: 3% Eu3+ powder was thoroughly dispersed in 15 ml DI water and then stored at 4 °C.

The N-CDs were prepared according to the previous report with a little modification [10]. Briefly, 2 mmol CA was placed in 10 ml water under stirring for 30 min, then 540 µL of EDA was added. By further stirring for 30 min, the above mixture with 20 ml DI water was transferred to 100 ml autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, 15 ml of N-CDs (1 mmol) crude solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (300 Da) against DI water for 24 h. As a result, due to reverse osmosis of DI water outside the dialysis bag, its volume increased from 15 to 20 ml. From Fig. S1, one can see that the synthesized N-CDs crude solution is brown-red and semitransparent, while the color of dialyzed one turns primrose yellow. The final products were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

The composite material GdVO4: 3% Eu3+/c(d)CDs was synthesized with hydrothermal deposition method. Typically, 0.12 g of GdVO4: 3% Eu3+ (denoted as GVE) was dispersed in 10 ml DI water, then 15 (20) ml crude (dialyzed) CDs solution (1 mmol) was added. After kept stirring for 4 h at room temperature, 10 (5) ml DI water was added to the above homogeneous suspension, then the suspension was heated at 140 °C for 4 h in a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the resultant GVE/c(d)CDs was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rmp for 8 min and washed with DI water and ethyl alcohol in turn three times. Finally, the composite GVE/c(d)CDs was stored in 15 ml of DI water at 4 °C.

For convenient comparison, the GVE/c(d)CDs-m and GVE/CDs-h composites were also synthesized, where c (d) denotes the crude (dialyzed) CDs solution used as precursor, and m (h) means the physical mixing (hydrothermal) process. For example, GVE/cCDs-m means that the composite can be prepared by firstly physical mixing the nanocrystals GVE and crude CDs solution and then stirring at room temperature overnight. The symbolic representation of remaining samples can be interpreted similarly.

To investigate the influence of different dosages of ammonia aqueous solutions on emission intensities of the composites, GVE/c(d)CDs(x) was also prepared, where x means the dosages of ammonia added in the precursor. For GVE/cCDs composite material, x = 0, 2 or 5 ml. As for GVE/dCDs, x = 0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5 or 1 ml. Detailed synthesized procedure for these composites can be found at supporting information. By comparing the above synthetic route of GVE/cCDs with that of GVE/CDs-h shown in the support information, one can see that the main difference between the two routes is that GVE/CDs-h can be prepared by one-step synthesis method with citrate anions, ethylenediamine cations and polymer-like CDs as precursor, while GVE/cCDs requires two-step synthesis with synthesized crude CDs solution used as the precursor.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were analyzed by using a Bruker D8 Focus diffractometer with a graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15496 nm) at a step rate of 0.02° in the 2θ range 10°-80° (or 10°-90°). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) were performed using a Tecnai G2 F20. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed using Thermo Escalab 250Xi. UV-Vis absorption spectra were measured with a Hitachi U-3310 UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a NICOLET 460 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a Horiba FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp source. The photographs under daylight and UV irradiation were taken using a Nikon D750 digital camera with the scene model. All measurements were performed at room temperature.

Fig. 1 presents the proposed synthesis procedure of GVE/cCDs composite material and its anti-counterfeiting application. Firstly, the GVE nanocrystals and N-CDs are prepared with the hydrothermal method, respectively. Then, the as-prepared GVE nanocrystals are annealed at 600 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, the annealed GVE nanocrystals and N-CDs crude solution are simultaneously dispersed in DI water with continuous stirring for 4 h. After hydrothermal reaction at 140 °C for 4 h, the GVE/cCDs composite can be formed. The GVE/cCDs ink comprises a certain volume of alcohol, PAA aqueous solution and the nanocrystals, in which the distribution of luminescent particles is uniform. The printed pattern is invisible under the daylight, but can present bright red (blue) color under the irradiation of 254 (365) nm UV light.

The morphology and microstructure of the annealed GVE and GVE/cCDs were investigated by electron microscopy. Fig. 2a exhibits that the annealed GVE nanocrystals with average size of about 30 nm are almost ellipsoid, which are aggregated apparently due to the nanocrystals annealed at 600 °C for 5 h, and the particle shown in the dashed-line box section is semitransparent. The clear lattice structure in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2b) denotes that the particles are highly crystallized. Lattice spacing of the structure is about 0.479 nm, agreeing well with (101) lattice planes of the tetragonal-phase GdVO4 crystals. By comparing images shown in Fig. 2a and c, one can deduce that the dark sections, which are partially marked with dashed yellow line, indicate the CDs successfully coupled with the GVE nanocrystals. The dense dark dots in Fig. 2d are partially marked with small yellow circles, further demonstrating the CDs successfully combined with the nanocrystals. HRTEM image manifests that the prepared nanocrystals are the tetragonal-phase GdVO4 crystals (Fig. 2e). In the figure, the diffraction patterns of carbon dots can’t be determined, suggesting the synthesized CDs are amorphous. In Fig. 2f, EDX spectrum indicates that the components of GVE/cCDs composite material are Gd, Eu, V, O, C and N elements, which are consistent with the synthetic scheme. The remarkable diffraction peak of C element denotes that a tremendous amount of CDs are successfully combined with the nanocrystals.

The corresponding X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples dCDs, GVE and GVD/cCDs and standard card data of GdVO4 crystal are provided in Fig. 2g. The pattern of dCDs exhibits a broad peak centered at about 27.7°, which is ascribed to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic structures [19,20]. The broad peak indicates that the prepared CDs contain more amorphous structure, which is consistent with the TEM results. For the samples GVE and GVD/cCDs, all of the diffraction peaks can be readily indexed to pure tetragonal phased GdVO4, and locations and relative intensities of the diffraction peaks fit well with the standard card (PDF No. 17-0260) without any impurity phase [21]. In the figure, robust and sharp diffraction peaks of GVE can be seen due to the sample having been annealed at 600 °C for 5 h. On coating with CDs, corresponding intensities decrease to some extent without emergence of CDs diffraction peak.

In addition, TEM images and XRD patterns of the composites GVE/cCDs-m and GVE/CDs-h were investigated as shown in Fig. S2. Obviously, the dark sections partially marked with dashed yellow line indicate that the N-CDs are successfully coupled with the GVE nanocrystals. The XRD spectra are similar to those presented in Fig. 2g, which reveal that the intensities of corresponding diffraction peaks decrease accordingly due to the coated CDs.

UV-Vis absorption and XPS spectra of corresponding samples were also recorded as shown in Fig. 3. The absorption spectrum of dCDs solution shown in Fig. 3a exhibits two evident absorption peaks centered at 238 and 345 nm, which correspond to the π→ π * transition of CC and the n→ π * transition of the two function groups of CO (4.1 eV, 300 nm) and CN (2.9 eV, 421 nm), respectively [14,22]. For the GVE aqueous solution, two strong absorption bands at about 240 and 305 nm originates from charge transfer band (CTB) between V5+ and O2-. These strong absorption bands in UV region come from Vanadate (VO4)3- group, which acts as self-activated host [23,24]. On coating with the N-CDs, CTB absorption bands of the GVE/cCDs composite turn more robust and narrower. These remarkable differences demonstrate that N-CDs are responsible for modifying the surface of GVE nanocrystals, resulting in the formation of GVE/cCDs composite material. In addition, UV-Vis absorption spectra of GVE/cCDs-m and GVE/CDs-h are presented in Fig. S3 . By comparing the spectra shown in the figure, it can be seen that the coated CDs make the absorption peak more stronger. For GVE/CDs-h, the narrowing of absorption peak comes from two aspects: the coated CDs and a large number of citrate ions presented on the surfaces of GVE nanocrystals (see Fig. S6b).

The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3b) display that the samples GVE and GVE/cCDs have the peaks around 141.5, 284.8, 399.8, 517, 530 and 1134 eV corresponding to Gd4d, C1s, N1s, V2p, O1s and Eu3d, respectively. For the GVE phosphor, the negligible peak intensity of N1s stems from nitrogen in air during high temperature thermal annealing process. Compared with those of GVE phosphor, the related intensities of element peaks of GVE/cCDs are distinctly decreased except C1s and N1s elements. On the contrary, the peak intensities of C1s and N1s are significantly enhanced on coating with the N-CDs. All of these results denote that N-CDs are successfully coated on the surface of GVE nanocrystals. Fig. 3c and d exhibit the C1s spectra of the samples GVE and GVE/cCDs, respectively. As shown in the spectra, the two samples show three peaks at 284.7, 286.1 and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to C-C/CC, C-N, and CO/CN, respectively [14,25]. From the two figures, one can see that the proportion of C-N increases from 15.9% to 28.9%, signifying N-CDs effectively combined with the GVE nanocrystals.

To investigate the formation mechanism and fluorescence enhancement reason of the composite material GVE/cCDs, we further analyzed the XPS spectra of other composite materials, such as GVE/dCDs and GVE/cCDs-m. The atomic ratios of Gd, C, N, V, O, Eu in XPS results are shown in Table 1. As demonstrated in the table, the composites GVE/cCDs and GVE/dCDs have higher carbon atomic ratios, which means more CDs are deposited on surfaces of the nanoparticles via hydrothermal deposition process, though GVE/c(d)CDs owns the highest (lowest) nitrogen atomic ratio. In order to facilitate comparison, the XPS survey spectra of GVE/cCDs, GVE/dCDs and GVE/cCDs-m are supplied in Fig. S4a. Notably, the XPS intensities of element peaks are various for the samples, implying that the concentrations of related elements are different. As shown in the figure, the sample GVE/cCDs exhibits highest peak intensities of C1s and N1s, whereas GVE/cCDs-m shows lowest peak intensity of C1s and weaker peak intensity of N1s. By comparing the results in Fig. S4b and c, one can see that the proportion of C-N decreases from 28.9% to 15.9%, which means dramatic reduction in nitrogen content of GVE/dCDs. Whereas, the proportion of C-N of GVE/cCDs-m is comparable to that of GVE/dCDs (Fig. S4d). The reasonable explanation for this phenomenon is that utilization of CDs crude solution as precursor causes a large amount of citrate ions (Cit-) attached on surface of the GVE/cCDs-m nanocrystals, resulting in the proportion of C-C (C-N) increased (decreased) sharply, which will be validated via the following FT-IR spectra.

The High-resolution XPS N1s spectra of GVE/cCDs, GVE/dCDs and GVE/cCDs-m are supplied in Fig. S5. The N1s spectra show three peaks at about 399.6, 401.4 and 407 eV, which are attributed to the C-N, N-H and -NO3 (nitrate residue), respectively [26,27]. Obviously, the relatively high proportion of N-H shown in Fig. S5a and c could be from the amidogen which residents in the crude CDs solution, which will be validated via the FT-IR spectra. The very weak peak intensities of N1s shown in Fig. S5b denote that the nitrogen content of GVE/dCDs is very low, which is consistent with the results shown in Table S1. As demonstrated in the table, the peak intensity of C-N of GVE/cCDs is 7.2 -fold in contrast to that of GVE/dCDs.

To illustrate if there exist the ingredients of CA and EDA in the dCDs and cCDs aqueous solution, the FT-IR spectra of citrate acid powder and c(d)CDs solution were investigated in Fig. 4a. The figure reveals the following vibrational absorption bands: O-H (3372 cm−1), CO (1688–1758 cm−1), N-H (1649 cm−1), C-O (1256–1319 cm−1) and C-C (1076–1212 cm−1) [3,14,22,28,29]. For the N-CDs crude solution, the notable absorption bands of CO, C-O, C-C and N-H denote that the ingredients of citrate acid and ethylenediamine retain abundantly. In contrast, the weak counterparts of dialyzed CDs solution signify those corresponding ingredients are almost cleaned up during dialysis process. In addition, the FT-IR spectra of GVE and GVE/c(d)CDs were also measured as shown in Fig. 4a. In the figure, strong absorption bands centered at 803 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational mode of V-O bond, and the sharp peaks at 450 cm−1 correspond to Gd (Eu)-O absorption [30,31]. Obviously, the coated N-CDs narrow the broad V-O absorption band, which means a portion of vibrational energy of V-O bond has been transferred to the coated CDs. It is observed that coating more CDs on surface of the nanoparticles make the absorption band narrower. The FT-IR spectra furtherly confirm that N-CDs are integrated with GVE nanocrystals. It is worth noting that compared to that of GVE, the absorption spectra of GVE/cCDs and GVE/dCDs at 1400–1600 cm−1 do not change much. Apparently, this is because the resultant GVE/cCDs and GVE/dCDs were centrifuged and washed three times in sequence with DI water and ethanol, resulting in citrate anions and ethylenediamine cations almost being cleaned up.

FT-IR spectra of GVE/cCDs-m and GVE/CDs-h are presented in Fig. S6a. For GVE/cCDs-m, the coated N-CDs narrow the V-O absorption band slightly because of relatively small number of N-CDs deposited. The notable absorption bands of CO and O-H denote that citrate ions are abundant on the surfaces of GVE nanocrystals, which can be confirmed by Fig. S6b. In the figure, the brown appearance of GVE/CDs-h under daylight indicates that the surfaces of GVE nanocrystals are abundantly coated with citrate ions.

Based on the analysis results of FT-IR spectra, we can explain the above XPS results as follows: for production yield of the N-CDs is ca. 58%, which was reported in Ref. [10], one can speculate that there are a large amount of citrate anions, ethylenediamine cations and polymer-like CDs in the crude CDs solution. The ions and polymer-like CDs make the crude solution as nutrient solution of CDs, which can not only facilitate growth of CDs on the surface of GVE, but also greatly prevents the nitrogen atoms from falling off the particles. Whereas, for the dialyzed CDs solution, the ions and polymer-like CDs were thoroughly dialyzed against DI water, making the coated CDs unprotected (the difference of cCDs and dCDs solutions can be seen in Fig. S1). During hydrothermal deposition process, the CDs are fragile under high temperature and pressure conditions, resulting in a lot of nitrogen atoms fallen off the CDs. As a consequence, highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/CDs composite material can be synthesized via crude CDs solution rather than the dialyzed one.

The PL emission spectra of GVE and its composites with λex = 254 and 365 nm are supplied in Fig. 4b and c, respectively. To ensure emission intensities of six samples are comparable, 0.2 ml dispersed solution of each sample was diluted with 2.5 ml DI water, then the diluted solutions were used for spectral test. Upon excitation at 254 nm, the six samples exhibit the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ ions, consisting of 5D0 → 7F1 (591 nm), 5D0 →7F2 (617 nm), 5D0 → 7F3 (649 nm) and 5D0 → 7F4 (701 nm) transitions [32–34]. From Fig. 4b, one can see that emission intensities of Eu3+ decline accordingly when the GVE nanocrystals are coated with CDs. For convenience, the six samples are labeled as follows: GVE (S1), GVE/dCDs (S2), GVE/dCDs-m (S3), GVE/cCDs-m (S4), GVE/cCDs (S5), GVE/CDs-h (S6). In the inset of Fig. 4b, the relative total emission intensities (RTE) of Eu3+ of the six samples are presented, in which RTE is defined as the total relative peak area corresponding to 5D0 → 7Fj (j = 1–4) transitions. For the sample S2, its value of RTE is 0.88, whereas the value of S5 is 0.7. Obviously, the reduction of Eu3+ emission intensity comes from the absorption of UV excitation light by the coated CDs. Generally, the more CDs are coated, the stronger the absorption of UV light is. As for S6, it can be inferred that rich citrate ions coated on the surfaces of GVE nanocrystals further reduce Eu3+ emission, as shown in Fig. S6b and Fig. 6 (see below).

Emission spectra of the six samples with λex = 365 nm were recorded as shown in Fig. 4c. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, S5 exhibits highest emission intensity of CDs, which is 2.5, 3.8 and 9.5 -fold in contrast to that of S4, S6 and S3, respectively. Based on the above analyses, we can explain the obvious emission differences of the six samples as: during hydrothermal deposition process, depositing more N-CDs on the surfaces of GVE nanocrystals without obvious shedding of nitrogen atoms result in highest emission intensity of S5; severe shedding of nitrogen atoms causes S2 show negligible emission intensity of CDs; for samples prepared by stirring at room temperature, relatively small amount of N-CDs are deposited on the surfaces of nanocrystals, while a certain number of citrate ions presented in the crude CDs solution promote the deposition of CDs on the surfaces of nanocrystals, these two factors cause S3 and S4 exhibit moderate emission intensity of CDs; during hydrothermal process, a large amount of citrate anions coated on the surfaces of nanocrystals are hard to efficiently convert to CDs, resulting in a relatively small amount of N-CDs deposition, in addition, the coated citrate anions also reduce the emission of N-CDs to a certain extent. Fig. 4d presents the photographs of GVE/cCDs solution under excitation of 254, 365 and 254 and 365 nm UV light, respectively. The pink color fluorescence demonstrates that emission intensity of CDs under 365 nm irradiation is comparable to that of Eu3+ ions under excitation of 254 nm.

The CIE chromaticity coordinates for GVE and its composites can be determined on the basis of their corresponding PL spectra ( Table 2 and Fig. 5). It can be seen from Fig. 5 that under excitation of 365 nm, the colors for the samples S4, S5 and S6 in CIE chromaticity coordinates are blue due to their strong blue light emission. As for the samples S1, S2 and S3, one can see that their emission color in CIE chromaticity coordinates tend to be more red accompany by the sharp decrease in blue emission intensity. Under excitation of 254 nm, the emission color of all samples in CIE chromaticity coordinates is red, and their CIE coordinate denotes at A is about (0.6655, 0.3342).

The effects of different dosages of ammonia on emission intensities of the two composites were also studied. As shown in Fig. S7, the addition of ammonia in the precursors have little effect on Eu3+ emission of GVE/cCDs, but cause emission intensities of the coated CDs declined dramatically. A reasonable explanation for this phenomenon is that the addition of ammonia leads to the amounts of coated CDs decreased significantly. For the GVE/dCDs (x) composite materials, where x = 0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5 and 1 ml, their emission spectra were recorded in Fig. S8. Interestingly, one can see that under excitation of 254 nm emission intensities of Eu3+ ions are declined evidently when x ≥ 0.2 (Fig. S8a). Obviously, the occurrence of this phenomenon is linked to the CDs coated on surface of GVE nanocrystals, which can be observed in Fig. S8b. In the figure, the CDs emission in the range of 400–550 nm increases evidently when x ≥ 0.2, and reach the maximum value at about x = 0.3. The results mean that the addition of ammonia enables the coated CDs to partially restore their ability of light absorption and emission, though the effectiveness is very limited. The occurrence of this phenomenon should be caused by the NH3 + attaching on the surface of CDs, which makes the coated CDs partially restore their ability of light absorption and emission.

Finally, the dual-mode luminescent colorless inks were prepared by uniformly dispersing the composites GVE/cCDs, GVE/cCDs-m and GVE/CDs-h into the mixture of PAA and alcohol, respectively. Typically, 5 ml aqueous solution of the composite material were firstly dispersed in 4 ml PAA by ultrasonic action, then the mixture was heated at 60 ℃ for 3 h in a drying oven to evaporate the excess water, yielding a viscous liquid, subsequently 3 ml ethyl alcohol was added into the mixture, after stirring for 5 min, the well-dispersed colorless ink was finally prepared. To demonstrate that the composite material GVE/cCDs can be used for dual-mode fluorescent anti-counterfeiting, a designable pattern, the logo of “Nanchang Institute of Technology”, is shown in Fig. 6a. In ambient lighting condition, the logo is almost invisible. As we expected, under the excitation of 254 and 365 nm, it can be obviously observed red and blue color by naked eyes in Fig. 6a2 and a3, respectively. When both 254 and 365 nm UV lights of the portable ultraviolet lamp are on, pink color emission of the pattern can be observed by naked eyes (Fig. 6a4).

As for the composites GVE/cCDs-m and GVE/CDs-h, the designable patterns are shown in Fig. 6b and c. The patterns are also invisible under daylight, and show bright red-light emission under the excitation of 254 nm (Fig. 6b2 and c2), but they are illegible under the excitation of 365 nm (Fig. 6b3 and c3). When both 254 and 365 nm UV lights were on, red-light emission of the patterns can be observed by naked eyes. The above results make the composites GVE/cCDs-m and GVE/CDs-h out of the optimal choices for dual-mode fluorescent anti-counterfeiting. On the contrary, the composite material GVE/cCDs becomes one of the strong candidates for two-mode anti-counterfeiting.

In conclusion, the highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal deposition method. By virtue of the XPS and FT-IR spectra, we can deduce that the existence of a large amount of citrate anions, ethylenediamine cations and polymer-like CDs in the crude CDs solution make it as nutrient solution of CDs, which can not only facilitate growth of CDs on the surface of GVE, but also greatly prevent nitrogen atoms from falling off the particles during the hydrothermal deposition process. Whereas, for the dialyzed CDs solution, the ions and polymer-like CDs were thoroughly dialyzed against DI water, making the coated CDs unprotected. During hydrothermal deposition process, the CDs are fragile under high temperature and pressure conditions, resulting in a lot of nitrogen atoms fallen off the CDs. As a consequence, highly luminescent GdVO4:Eu3+/CDs composite material can be synthesized via crude CDs solution rather than the dialyzed one. The above scheme can be used for synthesizing other composites, hence our results provide a general approach to preparing highly luminescent composites. Besides, the effects of different dosages of ammonia on emission intensities of the composites were also studied. Finally, the dual-mode luminescent colorless inks were prepared by uniformly dispersing the composite in the mixture of PAA and alcohol. The designable pattern prepared with highly luminescent composite shows that the composite is one of the most promising candidates in the anti-counterfeiting field.

Zhan Shu: Experimental preparing of GVE nanocrystals and its composites, Writing – original draft. MengQi Li: Experimental preparing of N-CDs, Samples testing. Lu Gan: Preparing anti-counterfeiting ink and Patterns, Data processing. Lijia Wan: Program design, Theoretical guidance. Xiaoling Chen: Resources, Supervision. Xiaohang Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Weijun Gui: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61961027) and the College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Jiangxi Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.165881.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
